entry.title: Why SAP Signavio Reveals Process Issues That Traditional SAP Projects Often Miss
entry.content:
Why SAP Signavio Reveals Process Issues That Traditional SAP Projects Often Miss

Introduction

One lesson I have learned from working on SAP transformation programs is that technology problems are often easier to solve than process problems.

Most SAP projects invest significant effort in system configuration, data migration, testing, and cutover planning. These activities are critical for a successful implementation. However, many organizations still encounter operational challenges after go-live, even when the technical deployment itself is successful.

In many cases, the root cause is not the SAP system. It is the underlying business process.

Over the years, I have seen situations where different regions followed different approval paths, procurement processes varied between business units, and operational teams developed workarounds that were never formally documented. These variations often remain hidden until transformation programs attempt to standardize processes across the organization.

This is where SAP Signavio can provide significant value.

The Challenge with Traditional Process Discovery

Historically, process discovery relied heavily on workshops, interviews, documentation reviews, and process flow diagrams.

While these methods remain important, they also have limitations.

Business users may describe how a process is supposed to work rather than how it actually works. Documentation may be outdated. Regional teams may follow different procedures despite having the same documented process.

As a result, organizations often design future-state processes based on assumptions rather than actual execution data.

When these assumptions prove incorrect, issues begin to surface during testing, user adoption, or after go-live.

How SAP Signavio Changes the Conversation

What makes SAP Signavio different is its ability to analyze real operational data and provide visibility into how processes are actually executed across the organization.

Instead of relying solely on interviews and workshops, process mining helps organizations identify:

  • Process bottlenecks

  • Approval delays

  • Rework activities

  • Process variations

  • Compliance deviations

  • Manual workarounds

  • Inefficient handoffs between teams

This creates a fact-based view of operational reality rather than a theoretical view of how processes should work.

In my experience, this visibility often uncovers opportunities that traditional process assessments fail to identify.

Traditional Process Discovery vs Process Mining with SAP Signavio

Example: Procurement Process Variations

In one transformation initiative, procurement processes appeared standardized on paper.

However, when transaction data was analyzed, significant differences emerged across regions.

Some purchase requests moved through three approval levels while others required six. Certain business units consistently bypassed automated workflows and relied on manual interventions. Lead times varied considerably despite similar business requirements.

Without process mining, these variations would likely have remained hidden until much later in the transformation program.

By identifying these differences early, the organization was able to simplify approval paths, reduce unnecessary complexity, and establish more consistent operating procedures.

Illustrative Procurement Process Variations Identified Through SAP Signavio

Illustrative example showing how SAP Signavio process mining can identify approval bottlenecks, process variations, and manual workarounds across regions. Sample process flows are provided for educational purposes only.

Why Process Standardization Matters

One of the primary goals of many SAP S/4HANA programs is process standardization.

Organizations often seek to:

  • Improve efficiency

  • Reduce operational risk

  • Increase compliance

  • Simplify support models

  • Improve reporting consistency

  • Enable scalable growth

However, standardization becomes difficult when the organization lacks visibility into existing process variations.

SAP Signavio helps establish that visibility.

By understanding how work actually flows through the business, organizations can make informed decisions about which processes should be standardized and where flexibility remains necessary.

Supporting Successful S/4HANA Transformations

Many organizations view process mining as a separate initiative.

I believe its greatest value comes when it is integrated directly into SAP transformation programs.

When used early in the project lifecycle, SAP Signavio can help teams:

  • Establish process baselines

  • Identify improvement opportunities

  • Reduce process complexity

  • Improve business readiness

  • Support change management activities

  • Increase confidence in future-state designs

This reduces the risk of discovering major process issues during testing or after go-live.

SAP Signavio Supporting SAP S4HANA Transformation Programs

Illustrative example showing how SAP Signavio process mining supports SAP S/4HANA transformation programs by identifying bottlenecks, enabling process optimization, improving compliance, and driving better business outcomes.

Beyond Technology

One misconception is that SAP transformations fail because of technology limitations.

In reality, many transformation challenges originate from process inconsistencies, unclear ownership, or operational inefficiencies that existed long before the project began.

Technology can enable improvement, but it cannot automatically correct broken processes.

Tools such as SAP Signavio help organizations understand those processes before large-scale changes are introduced.

That understanding often becomes one of the most valuable outcomes of the transformation itself.

Conclusion

From my experience, some of the most important discoveries in SAP transformation programs come from understanding how the business actually operates rather than how it believes it operates.

SAP Signavio provides organizations with a data-driven approach to process visibility, helping uncover bottlenecks, variations, and inefficiencies that traditional assessments may overlook.

As organizations continue investing in SAP S/4HANA and digital transformation initiatives, process transparency will become increasingly important. The earlier process issues are identified, the easier they are to address, and the greater the likelihood of achieving sustainable transformation outcomes.

entry.title: Building a Business-Critical Readiness Score for SAP S/4HANA Transformations
entry.content:
Building a Business-Critical Readiness Score for SAP S/4HANA Transformations

In many SAP S/4HANA programs, teams spend a lot of time tracking tasks, milestones, and project plans. On paper, everything may look fine. But when cutover gets closer, the real question is not whether the work is technically “done.” The real question is whether the business is actually ready.

That is where a readiness score can help.

A readiness score gives transformation teams a practical way to measure how prepared a program really is. Instead of relying only on status updates or gut feel, it creates a simple way to look at the health of the transformation across business, data, integration, testing, and cutover. In my experience, this kind of view is often what separates a smooth go-live from a painful one.

SAP S/4HANA Transformation Readiness Dashboard

Illustrative example of a transformation readiness dashboard showing key readiness indicators across business, data, testing, integration, and cutover domains.

Why project progress is not the same as readiness

One of the biggest mistakes in large SAP programs is assuming that progress automatically means readiness. A team may have completed design workshops, built the technical solution, and even started testing, but still be far from ready to go live. The reason is simple: transformation success depends on much more than completed tasks.

For example, the project may look healthy from a schedule perspective, but master data could still be inconsistent. Interfaces may work in the development environment but fail under real business volumes. Business users may not yet trust the new process. Cutover activities may still depend on too many manual steps. None of these issues always show up clearly in a standard project dashboard.

A readiness score helps expose those gaps early. It creates a more honest picture of whether the organization can actually move into production with confidence.

What a readiness score should cover

A useful readiness score should reflect the full transformation picture, not just one part of it. If it only focuses on testing, then data issues and organizational concerns may get missed. If it only focuses on data, then integration and cutover risks may be underestimated. The score needs to bring all the important pieces together.

The main areas I would include are:

  • Business process fit.

  • Master data quality.

  • Integration stability.

  • Test completeness.

  • Cutover preparedness.

  • User and organizational readiness.

  • Support and hypercare readiness.

Illustrative readiness scorecard used to evaluate critical transformation domains before go-live decisions.

Each of these areas matters because a weakness in any one of them can create serious problems at go-live. A program is only as strong as its weakest readiness area.

A simple way to score it

The best readiness models are usually the easiest to understand. I prefer a simple scoring scale, such as 0 to 5 for each domain, with weights based on business importance. Then the total can be converted into a 100-point readiness index that leadership can interpret quickly.

For example:

 
Area What it tells you
Business process fitWhether the future process truly supports how the business works.
Master data qualityWhether the data is clean and ready for migration.
Integration stabilityWhether interfaces and messages are behaving reliably.
Test completenessWhether the key business scenarios have been validated.
Cutover preparednessWhether the move to production can happen with control.
User readinessWhether people are prepared for the new process.
Support readinessWhether the team can handle issues after go-live.

Once this is scored, the program can use a simple interpretation such as:

  • Above 85: strong readiness.

  • 70 to 85: moderate risk, but manageable with active mitigation.

  • Below 70: significant concern and likely not ready for go-live.

The exact numbers can vary, but the point is to make readiness visible and easy to act on.

What this looks like in real life

Let’s say a transformation program is approaching cutover. The team feels confident because testing is mostly complete and the technical build is done. But once the readiness score is updated, a different picture appears. Data quality is still weak, a few critical interfaces have unstable behavior, and the business has not fully signed off on the future process.

That score changes the conversation.

Instead of asking, “Are we on track?”, leadership starts asking, “What do we need to fix before we can safely go live?” That shift is important. It moves the program from activity tracking to risk management.

In a complex SAP environment, that is often the difference between success and rework.

Why AI can improve the score

AI can make readiness scoring even more useful by turning it from a static checklist into a predictive tool. Instead of waiting for issues to become visible in late testing or cutover rehearsal, AI can help identify patterns earlier.

Illustrative example showing how AI-driven insights can identify emerging risks and improve transformation readiness visibility.

For example, repeated migration errors may signal data quality issues. A spike in defects in one process area may indicate that testing coverage is incomplete. Frequent manual workarounds during cutover simulation may show that the go-live plan is too fragile. These are all signals that can be captured and used to improve the readiness score.

This is where the combination of human judgment and predictive insight becomes powerful. The business provides context, and the data provides early warning.

Why governance matters

A readiness score only works if people actually use it. It should not be treated as a one-time presentation slide. It needs to become part of the program’s regular governance rhythm.

Illustrative governance framework showing how readiness scoring can support operational, program, and executive decision-making.

A good approach is to review it at three levels:

  • Workstream level, where owners update their areas honestly and regularly.

  • Program level, where trends and risks are discussed across teams.

  • Executive level, where the score supports go/no-go decisions.

The most important thing is consistency. If each team scores itself differently, the model loses value. But if everyone uses the same rules and the same definitions, the score becomes a trusted decision tool.

Common mistakes to avoid

There are a few traps I have seen in transformation programs.

The first is making the score too complicated. If it takes too long to update or explain, people stop trusting it. The score should be simple enough to maintain but strong enough to reflect real risk.

The second is measuring activity instead of readiness. Completing a task is not the same as being ready. A test case closed does not mean the business process works. A migration file loaded does not mean the data is correct. Leadership should always look for evidence of readiness, not just evidence of effort.

The third is leaving the business out of the conversation. SAP transformation is not only a technical exercise. If the business does not validate the future process, if users are not prepared, or if support ownership is unclear, the program is still exposed.

A practical takeaway

If you are leading an SAP S/4HANA transformation, I would strongly recommend building a readiness score early in the program. It does not need to be perfect. It just needs to be honest, consistent, and useful.

A simple scorecard can prevent a lot of avoidable trouble. It helps leadership see risks before they become incidents. It helps teams focus on what matters most. And it gives the entire program a clearer picture of whether it is truly ready for the next step.

In large transformations, that kind of clarity is valuable. Sometimes the difference between a successful go-live and a difficult one is not effort — it is visibility.

entry.title: 4th SAP Stammtisch Cyprus: Concentrated Know-How on the Island Once Again
entry.content:
Once again the SAP community came together in Cyprus, and once again there was a lot of knowledge in the room. Two questions were at the center of the evening.

Question 1: When is custom code worth it?

The group largely agreed on the basics: a custom development pays off above all when it noticeably increases the quality or the speed of a process, or when a requirement simply cannot be mapped sensibly within the standard.

Particularly interesting was the discussion about what actually counts as "SAP standard." Is it only customizing via the IMG, or do extensions through BAdIs and similar intended mechanisms count as well? Here the definitions diverged, and that is exactly what made the exchange so valuable.

Question 2: AI in combination with SAP

Here it became clear that broad practical experience is still limited. First touchpoints came, for example, through the free version of the Public Cloud.

One concrete and promising real-world example was shared, and notably independent of Joule or the Public Cloud: invoices or purchase orders that arrive by email are processed by an AI and then automatically created as objects in SAP via an API, without any manual coordination. This works just as well with ECC or an on-premise system. Scenarios like this show where things could be heading.

Overall, AI is on everyone's lips. One open question remained: whether the Public Cloud should establish itself as the only form of SAP, since a good deal of flexibility would be lost along the way.

Conclusion

A successful evening with pragmatic exchange, honest assessments, and great company. We are already looking forward to the next time.

entry.title: Re: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
@rafledeganck 

Please find all queries you have posted

Question: ... only approved Attendance Quota are taken into account in the time valuations ?
I.e. it is not necessary to filter out pending Attendance Quota requests as it is for Single Record Approval ?

Ans: Only Approved attendance quota is taken into account in Time Valuation. If attendance quota is not approved, it is not considered. It is not necessary to filter Attendance Quota as only approved Attendance Quota are applicable for valuation processing.

Question: ... at this moment only the "Payout" option is supported for Attendance Quotas, and not "Time in Lieu" or "Payout + Time in Lieu" ?
Or does it follow the Default Overtime Compensation Variant in Job Info ?
I'm trying to understand what exactly is meant with the the last topic under "Future Enhancements", and which gap this will be closing, because our customer allows employees to choose the compensation type separately for each overtime request.

Ans: Currently, Default Overtime Compensation Variant in Job Info is used for the posting to Time off in lieu or Payout + time off in lieu. In future, we are planning to provide this as an option in Attendance Quota.

Question: Would you mind explaining in a bit more detail how you have configured the time type group "Payroll Relevant (PAYROLL_RLEVANT)" in your example ? (in the 5th time valuation "CLT_THRESHOLD_TIMESHEET_WEEK")

I have been configuring the attendance quotas as you explain above, but when I configure this time type group as a "Time Pay Type", the system throws the error "The group PAYROLL_RLEVANT is influenced by a time container and can therefore only produce alerts or a flexible-period time container type as a result."

Ans: Time Type group can be configured as below. There is one issue that either you will need to configure for Payroll or to TOIL account. You could also continue the chain and then post to the Time Off in Lieu.

entry.title: Unlocking the Practical Value of AI for Procurement: Autonomous Spend Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Over the years, Procurement has radically transformed from being “manual” to “digitised”, and now it has entered the exciting age of Artificial Intelligence (“AI”). There has never been an age with more powerful tools or greater opportunities for Procurement. However, there are some misconceptions about AI which need to be cleared up. This situation is not helped by the view that it is easy to simply put AI in the title of anything to make it sound relevant and/or interesting. Fortunately, this view is not universally shared. The reality is that AI is the only topic in the room whichever way you turn as the next-generation experience.

 

Recent research shows that large-scale digital transformation programs continue to fail most often because of organisational and operational issues rather than technology limitations. Common causes include poor governance, weak stakeholder alignment and unrealistic program scoping. At the same time, there is increasing pressure on organisations to demonstrate quick and measurable ROI from the investment in transformation programs. These challenges and issues also apply to AI.

AI is rapidly reshaping Procurement, but many teams are still asking the same question: “What really works in practice?”

 

Practical Value of AI for Procurement

Many use cases underpinned by proven examples emphasise the role of AI in enhancing productivity and operational efficiency by automating repetitive tasks such as purchase order creation, low-value RFx, and settling payment disputes. CPOs and their procurement teams can focus more on strategic activities including category strategy, supplier collaboration, and risk management. Further to this, AI brings capacity to tail spend management which falls below the threshold for Central Procurement teams for more effective control and driving cost savings. AI is not only innovative; it delivers which are incremental to what is achieved from current transformation programs.

For example, there is a clear use case for AI in category management. Data collection and category research are historically done manually. Strong market knowledge is needed, and stakeholder engagement is required. Fortunately, there is a better, faster way with AI in category management. It normally takes many weeks to manually create a strategy for a new purchasing category, but with AI embedded in category management, research on category specific information is available with a few clicks. Here SAP’s co-pilot, Joule, leverages information from the category initiative and uses historic sourcing data, including commodity code, sourcing event type and baseline spend to create the RFP. It also uses AI to recommend suppliers who meet the business criteria, like low carbon footprint and cost-saving considerations, whilst also including suppliers an organisation may have successfully worked with in the past. What was once days and weeks’ worth of work, can now be completed in a fraction of the time.

As demonstrated above, organisations can operate smarter, respond faster and free up capacity to work on the most value-adding strategic tasks.  What is needed is a clear roadmap for AI to build on, and align with, the overall digitalisation strategy for procurement. Also, there needs to be validated and compelling business case. This is where the true value of AI becomes a practical reality for CPOs.

Autonomous Spend Management

There is no doubt that AI matters in the world of Procurement. At Sapphire Orlando 2026, SAP unveiled the groundbreaking Autonomous Spend Management. This is a core pillar of the Autonomous Enterprise vision, which uses agentic AI to deliver continuity across procurement, travel, expenses, and external workforce processes. A new suite of Joule Assistants will support the full spend lifecycle, from category management and sourcing to invoicing and expense reporting.

Before we go any further, let us address the “elephant in the room”: AI does not in itself replace people. It amplifies human capability and does not replace human purpose. As with any transformation program, it is for the organisation to decide how it takes the benefits to the bottom line. Also, AI does not replace software. With Autonomous Spend, AI enhances and extends the capabilities of source to pay applications with agentic applications which are powered by specialised AI agents. Here, AI assistants and agents, with human oversight, manage and execute the end-to-end processes across source-to-pay. 

Digitalisation is closing the maturity gap, and AI is connecting digitalisation with autonomy. As an organisation matures along its transformation journey, the focus is set to move from basic digitalisation to true autonomy. This is shown in the following diagram.

Autonomous Spend Management builds upon the investment customers have already made in the earlier stages of their transformation. When engages AI with reliability, responsibility and relevance, then we expand the potential of Procurement far more than we diminish it. However, underlying source-to-pay processes cannot still be fragmented if you want to scale the value of AI and realise its true ROI.

 

Conclusion

AI in Procurement will continue to be the “great conversation”. If you have digitised your Procurement and have the foundations in place for further improvement and innovation, then AI will drive further benefits from your procurement transformation program:

 

  • Autonomous Spend Management builds upon the investment customers have already made in the earlier stages of their transformation.
  • AI enhances and extends the capabilities of source-to-pay applications with agentic applications which are powered by specialised AI agents.
  • AI does not in itself replace people, and AI does not replace software. SAP-built AI assistants and agents with human interaction is where the magic really happens for Procurement.

 

 

For further information

  • You can learn more about how AI is transforming Procurement by visiting: SAP Business AI for Procurement
  • See the blog from Etosha Thurman, Chief Marketing Officer for SAP Finance & Spend Management: Enabling Autonomous Spend Management with AI and Connected Processes

 

 

 

Frank Omare

Global Value Advisory, Spend Management

entry.title: Composable storefront issues for JDK21 upgrade
entry.content:
Onboarding JDK 21 

Following SAP Commerce Cloud upgrade policy , its essentials for customers to migrate to JDK 21.

There are multiple blogs , help document to bring you upto the speed on this upgrade & help you resolve common issues.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_CLOUD_PUBLIC_CLOUD/75d4c3895cb346008545900bffe851ce/9efd1f6212134dec8236a146cac4c98a.html

https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/building-a-custom-login-page-for-sap-commerce-composable-storefront-jdk21/ba-p/14337356

https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-cc-jdk-21-upgrade/ba-p/14282138

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_COMPOSABLE_STOREFRONT/eaef8c61b6d9477daf75bff9ac1b7eb4/7e237cc1245c43219e0a957a2cfa81c2.html#loio7e237cc1245c43219e0a957a2cfa81c2

 

Troubleshooting Composable Storefront Issues on JDK 21

I would go through some of the issues I have faced from CS pov during this upgrade. 
You are  on jdk21 & custom login page is enabled.

1. Infinite loop on Login 

Issue

On click of login your storefront goes into an infinite loop

infinite loop

 

 

 

 

 

Reason

You have defined a custom routing for login page as below

    provideConfig(<RoutingConfig>{
      routing: {
        routes: {
          login: {
            paths: ['login'],
            protected: false,
            authFlow: true,
          },
        },
      },
    }),
Solution
  • Post jdk21 upgrade , default login path is 'sign-in'
  • Remove/adjust your routing path 
  • Check standard code for the login route

2.  After login , user is again is redirected back to login page

Issue
  • Post logon details are filled & login is clicked , nothing happens.
  • No API failure detected in network

 

Reason
  • You have made custom implementation of class OAuthLibWrapperService & overridden tryLogin method like below
    override tryLogin(): Promise<OAuthTryLoginResult> {
    return new Promise((resolve) => {
      resolve({
        result: false,
        tokenReceived: false,
      });
    });
  }
Solution 
  • Check standard implementation of class OAuthLibWrapperService
  • tokenReceived & result should not be hardcoded.
  • token Received means if a user has completed a login redirect

3. Login failed

Issue
  • On click of login you get an error angular-oauth2-oidc - Error performing ${grantType} flow 

  • In network tab token call is still taking old parameters as payload

Reason
  • You have a custom implementation of login form component module & template is overridden.
  • OOTB login form component utilize nativeForm concept to enable form submit
  • Check login method of OOTB LoginFormComponentService
Solution
  • Revisit your template implementation 
  • Add view child decorator #loginForm. Check OOTB login-form.component.html

Cheers

K Rituraj

P.S- I would keep on updating list as I would encounter them

entry.title: Re: Calling SAP CAP API protected by SAP IAS from ABAP
entry.content:
How can this simple requirement of consuming CAP API from ABAP via technical user can be achieved? 

The easiest way is to grant access to APIs to the pseudo role system in CAP. 

For option 1, you have to setup an app-to-app scenario. Essentially you have to configure another application in IAS. That gives you a new client ID. You have to configure provider APIs in CAP application and configure dependency from this application to the CAP application' provider APIs in IAS. How to do this is detailed in SAP Help Documentation. 

For the option 2, you have to use grant type Resource Owner Password Credentials. If the profile supports it, configuration options should be available in SOAUTH2_CLIENT as mentioned in SAP Help Documentation.

Is there a good tutorial for this?

Not aware of any tutorials or step by step documentation.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I'd add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where Hyderabad SMBs lose the most money, and it's almost never a technical issue — it's a data readiness issue.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.
Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is "good enough" on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren't standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved. Real-time visibility into your data quality before cutover is non-negotiable — if you can't trust the numbers on Day 1, your team reverts to spreadsheets by Day 5.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.
SMB owners assume the consultant will "handle it." What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can't process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup. Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army. The goal is operational autonomy — your team should be able to pull live KPIs and act on them without calling the consultant.

3. No cutover rollback plan.
If something breaks on go-live night, what's the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you'd planned for it.

The go-live checklist that actually works:

  • Parallel run confirmation — both systems processing real transactions for at least 5 business days
  • Master data freeze — no item master or chart of accounts changes 48 hours before cutover
  • Batch traceability chain validated — for pharma/manufacturing, verify the genealogy from raw material batch → production order → FG batch is complete. A broken chain here means your first batch cost analysis is garbage
  • Rollback decision criteria documented — who decides, under what conditions, within what timeframe
  • Go-live support team on standby — internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight
  • First-week close check — finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days of go-live
  • Real-time dashboard validation — confirm your inventory KPIs, cashflow forecast, and batch cost reports are pulling live data correctly before you cut over. If your team can't see accurate numbers in real time on Day 1, the implementation has failed regardless of what the project plan says

Teams that skip this checklist average 3–4 weeks of post-go-live disruption. Teams that use it average 3–4 days.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation & AI-powered operations intelligence for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Expert Guidance Required – USA - Biweekly Timesheet with Weekly OT Calculation in SF Time Tracking
entry.content:
Dear All,

Hope you are doing well.

I would appreciate your expert opinion regarding a Time Tracking requirement for the USA.

Requirement Summary

  • Timesheet Submission Period: Biweekly (2 weeks)
  • Overtime Calculation: Weekly, based on hours worked from Monday through Sunday

I would like to understand the feasibility of configuring this requirement in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking and the recommended approach.

Scenario 1

When I configure:

  • Time Recording Approval Period: Length = 2 Weeks
  • Time Valuation Method: Valuate Whole Sheet or Period

the timesheet displays the following error:

"Time valuations with the valuation method 'Valuate Whole Sheet or Period' must be influenced by time containers if the time recording profile is used for flexible approval periods."

Scenario 2

When I change the valuation method to:

  • Valuate Per Period

I receive the following error:

"You can only use time collectors when the length of the approval period is set to Legacy Week. Instead of time collectors, you can configure time containers for all other approval periods."

Challenge

As part of my analysis, I observed that:

  • Time Collectors cannot be used with a 2-week approval period.
  • The system suggests using Time Containers instead.
  • However, within Time Containers, I am unable to define a weekly period (Monday–Sunday) or specify the start day of the week in the same way as Time Collectors.

Therefore, I am trying to determine whether the following requirement is supported:

Biweekly timesheet submission with overtime calculated independently for each week (Monday–Sunday) within the biweekly period.

Questions

  1. Is this requirement supported in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking?
  2. If supported, what is the recommended configuration approach?
  3. Are Flexible Periods in Time Valuation and Time Containers sufficient to achieve this requirement?
  4. If this is not currently supported due to product limitations, could you please share any relevant SAP documentation, KBA, or Help Portal references that can be used to explain the limitation to the client?

Any guidance, best practices, or references would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you for your help and support.

Best Regards,
Mahesh Dudam

entry.title: From Fragmented Landscapes to Unified Insights: Business Consent in SAP BDC
entry.content:
Organizations often discover that their SAP landscapes are spread across multiple CRM IDs just when they want to activate cross-solution scenarios like People Intelligence on SAP Business Data Cloud. This article walks through how Business Consent helps you overcome those CRM boundaries and quickly connect SAP BDC with say SAP SuccessFactors (any of our Public Cloud or RISE portfolio), without waiting for lengthy consolidation projects.

I would like to share some of the key learnings from SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) deep-dive design discussions and implementation experiences that may benefit customers and partners in the future.

Let us assume you are a customer using SAP SuccessFactors and are interested in exploring People Intelligence. You sign the order form for SAP BDC, and this order form grants you the necessary cloud services such as SAP BDC Core and includes a line item for People Intelligence, listing the number of users and the corresponding BDC Capacity Units to which you are entitled.

All these entitlements are grouped under a CRM ID, for example 100001. Since you already have SAP SuccessFactors, let us say it is under CRM ID 100002.

Once your SAP BDC tenant is provisioned, you proceed to add the relevant BDC components and the SuccessFactors system as part of the system formation (refer to these SAP BDC Provisioning blog series  by Shreya, where she provides an excellent explanation of the steps; Step 21 covers system formation). This is one of the mandatory steps required to deploy and use People Intelligence applications and their corresponding data products. When you deploy an app, system formation acts as the enabler for harmonizing data from the source system using SAP-managed pipelines, which in turn deploy data products and intelligent apps into BDC. For system formation, one of the expectations is that all landscapes, including the provisioned BDC tenant, fall under the same CRM ID. If this is not the case, you can still provision BDC and perform system formation for the BDC components, but since SuccessFactors is on 1000002, you will not be able to add it to the system formation.

When we review the order form and validate the CRM IDs, it is often the case that systems are distributed across multiple CRM IDs. While it is technically possible to consolidate all systems into a single CRM ID, this process may take several weeks or even months. This is precisely where the concept of Business Consent becomes valuable and helps you avoid that additional effort. Let me explain this with a scenario.

Based on the earlier example, from SAP4ME I have already added the BDC components as part of system formation, and these reside under CRM ID 100001.

Next, I attempt to include new systems in the system formation and search for the SuccessFactors system, but I am unable to find it because it is under a different CRM ID.

To have this system included in the system formation, I navigate to the "Consent Requests" section under the Customer Landscape tab in SAP4ME and then select "Request Consent".

You can either provide only the SuccessFactors system URL or specify the System ID and namespace, and then click "Request".

If you need to determine the System ID and namespace, here is an example of how I obtain them from the Systems Landscape under BTP for SuccessFactors. Namespaces will always follow the pattern sap.<cloudsystem>; in this case, it is sf. You then choose the scope as "System Sharing".

Once you click "Request", the consent request is sent to the BTP admin of the SuccessFactors landscape.

They can review the incoming consent request from their BTP Cockpit under the Consent Request section of the system landscape. Under the "Incoming Consent Requests" tab, the admin can either approve or reject the consent request.

After the request is approved, the BDC admin can view the status in SAP4ME under the customer landscape, where it will appear with the status "Approved".

At this point, the consent between BDC and the source system in a different CRM ID has been successfully established. When you now attempt to add the SuccessFactors system as part of the system formation, you will be able to find it, as the approved system from the consent request appears in the "Include Systems" list.

This approach eliminates the need to consolidate systems under a single CRM ID and still allows you to access systems that reside in different CRM IDs.

Business Consent offers a pragmatic path to realizing value from SAP Business Data Cloud even when your systems sit in different CRM IDs. By leveraging consent requests and system sharing, you can accelerate People Intelligence use cases, reduce landscape friction, and focus your energy on delivering insights rather than restructuring your commercial setup.

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge - Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content:
1. Parse the CloudEvent payload and Extract the data field
import pika, sys, os, json

def callback(ch, method, properties, body):
    print(" [x] Received ")
    print(f"{json.dumps(json.loads(body)["data"], indent=4, ensure_ascii=False)}")

def followTheWhiteRabbit():
    connection = pika.BlockingConnection(pika.ConnectionParameters('localhost'))
    channel = connection.channel()
    channel.queue_declare(queue='SAPDEV2026MAY', durable=True, arguments={'x-queue-type': 'quorum'})
    channel.basic_consume(queue='SAPDEV2026MAY',
                          auto_ack=True,
                          on_message_callback=callback)
    print(' [*] Waiting for messages. To exit press CTRL+C')
    channel.start_consuming()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        followTheWhiteRabbit()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print('Interrupted')
        try:
            sys.exit(0)
        except SystemExit:
            os._exit(0)
​

entry.title: Re: May Developer Challenge - Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload
entry.content:
Steps:

  1. Receive the event (as you did in Week 2), Extract the data field and prepare it as a string, Send that string to an embedding model and get back a vector:
import pika, sys, os, json
from ollama import embed

def createEmbedding(humanReadable):
    batch = embed(model='mistral',
                  input=[humanReadable])
    printTruncatedVector(batch['embeddings'])

def printTruncatedVector(vector):
    max=len(vector[0])
    print(" truncated embedding vector:")
    print(f"[{vector[0][0]},{vector[0][1]},{vector[0][2]}, ........... ,{vector[0][max-3]},{vector[0][max-2]},{vector[0][max-1]}]")

def callback(ch, method, properties, body):
    print("\n [x] Received\n")
    bpData = json.loads(body)["data"]
    humanReadable = (
        f"BusinessPartnerUUID: {bpData["BusinessPartnerUUID"]}"    
        f" BusinessPartner: {bpData["BusinessPartner"]}" 
        f" BusinessPartnerFullName: {bpData["BusinessPartnerFullName"]}"
        f" BusinessPartnerCategory: {bpData["BusinessPartnerCategory"]}"
        f" BusinessPartnerGrouping: {bpData["BusinessPartnerGrouping"]}"
        f" FirstName: {bpData["FirstName"]}"
        f" LastName: {bpData["LastName"]}"
        f" IsNaturalPerson: {bpData["IsNaturalPerson"]}"
        f" Country: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["Country"]}"
        f" Region: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["Region"]}"
        f" CityName: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["CityName"]}"
        f" PostalCode: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["PostalCode"]}"
        f" StreetName: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["StreetName"]}"
        f" HouseNumber: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["HouseNumber"]}"
        f" AddressTimeZone: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["AddressTimeZone"]}")
    print(f" human readable:\n{humanReadable}\n")
    createEmbedding(humanReadable)

def followTheWhiteRabbit():
    connection = pika.BlockingConnection(pika.ConnectionParameters('localhost'))
    channel = connection.channel()
    channel.queue_declare(queue='SAPDEV2026MAY', durable=True, arguments={'x-queue-type': 'quorum'})
    channel.basic_consume(queue='SAPDEV2026MAY',
                          auto_ack=True,
                          on_message_callback=callback)
    print(' [*] Waiting for messages. To exit press CTRL+C')
    channel.start_consuming()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        followTheWhiteRabbit()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print('Interrupted')
        try:
            sys.exit(0)
        except SystemExit:
            os._exit(0)
​
  • Log the vector (or a truncated version of it) to confirm it's working:

entry.title: Re: May Developer Challenge - Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG
entry.content:
1. A screenshot or code snippet showing Business Partner vectors being stored in your vector store,a sample query and the results returned (which Business Partners were retrieved?): i am now using chroma - so the embeddings are generated on document save, also there is an built in similarity search
import pika, sys, os, json, chromadb
from ollama import embed

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chromaDB')
collection = client.get_or_create_collection(name="sap_dev_may_2026")

def createAndStoreEmbedding(humanReadable, BPuuid):
    global collection
    collection.upsert(
            documents=[humanReadable],
            ids=[BPuuid])
    print("stored in chromadb")

def callback(ch, method, properties, body):
    print("\n [x] Received\n")
    bpData = json.loads(body)["data"]
    humanReadable = (
        f"BusinessPartnerUUID: {bpData["BusinessPartnerUUID"]}"    
        f" BusinessPartner: {bpData["BusinessPartner"]}" 
        f" BusinessPartnerFullName: {bpData["BusinessPartnerFullName"]}"
        f" BusinessPartnerCategory: {bpData["BusinessPartnerCategory"]}"
        f" BusinessPartnerGrouping: {bpData["BusinessPartnerGrouping"]}"
        f" FirstName: {bpData["FirstName"]}"
        f" LastName: {bpData["LastName"]}"
        f" IsNaturalPerson: {bpData["IsNaturalPerson"]}"
        f" Country: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["Country"]}"
        f" Region: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["Region"]}"
        f" CityName: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["CityName"]}"
        f" PostalCode: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["PostalCode"]}"
        f" StreetName: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["StreetName"]}"
        f" HouseNumber: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["HouseNumber"]}"
        f" AddressTimeZone: {bpData["BusinessPartnerAddress"]["AddressTimeZone"]}")
    print(f" human readable:\n{humanReadable}\n")
    createAndStoreEmbedding(humanReadable,bpData["BusinessPartnerUUID"])

def followTheWhiteRabbit():
 
    connection = pika.BlockingConnection(pika.ConnectionParameters('localhost'))
    channel = connection.channel()
    channel.queue_declare(queue='SAPDEV2026MAY', durable=True, arguments={'x-queue-type': 'quorum'})
    channel.basic_consume(queue='SAPDEV2026MAY',
                          auto_ack=True,
                          on_message_callback=callback)
    print(' [*] Waiting for messages. To exit press CTRL+C')
    channel.start_consuming()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        followTheWhiteRabbit()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print('Interrupted')
        try:
            sys.exit(0)
        except SystemExit:
            os._exit(0)
​
  • (Optional) The LLM-generated answer if you wired up the full RAG response:
import chromadb
from ollama import chat

chroma_client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chromaDB')

# switch \`create_collection\` to \`get_or_create_collection\` to avoid creating a new collection every time
collection = chroma_client.get_or_create_collection(name="sap_dev_may_2026")


def answerInHumanLanguage(collection, query):
    # Run the query
    result = collection.query(query_texts=[query],n_results=5)

    ListOfBpData = ""

    for ids, documents in zip(result["ids"], result["documents"]):
        for id, document in zip(ids, documents):
            ListOfBpData += f"{document}\n\n"
    
    prompt_text = f"""
Answer the following Question '{query}' abaout SAP Businesspartner data from the following context(List of SAP Businesspartner data).

RULES: 
 - never Answer just with UUIDs or IDs
 - Business Partner Fullname and BusinessPartner(ID) must be in every answer

CONTEXT: this is an list of Partners found bt cosine similarity from vectors( first places are most similar)

LIST OF SAP BUSINESS PARTNERS:
{ListOfBpData}
"""
    answers = chat(
               model='mistral',
               messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'{prompt_text}'}],
               stream=False )
    print(answers.message.content)
    

print("Ask questions about your SAP Business Partners. (Press Ctrl+C to exit)")
try:
    while True:
        # Get input from user
        query = input("\n❓ Your question: ").strip()

        # Skip empty inputs
        if not query:
            continue
        print("\n🔍 Results:")
        answerInHumanLanguage(collection, query)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("\n\n👋 Goodbye! Exiting loop.")
​

entry.title: Using SAP Joule with A2A from a custom agent on SAP BTP CF – any guidance or examples?
entry.content:
Hi all,

 

I am currently investigating how to use SAP Joule together with a custom agent running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry.

 

What I would like to achieve is the following:

 

- Build a custom agent on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

- Use A2A communication between my custom agent and SAP Joule

- Let SAP Joule handle specific SAP S/4HANA business operations, such as document creation or other standard S/4HANA transactions

- Keep the custom agent responsible for orchestration, while using Joule only for selected S/4HANA-related actions

 

I have found information about scenarios where Joule can call external/custom agents via A2A, but I am not sure whether the opposite direction is currently supported, meaning:

 

Custom Agent on BTP CF → A2A → SAP Joule → SAP S/4HANA operation

 

My questions are:

 

  1. Is this architecture currently supported or generally available?
  2. If yes, are there any official documents, tutorials, sample implementations, or reference architectures for this use case?

 

If this direction is not currently supported, I would also appreciate any recommended alternative architecture, for example using Joule as the entry point and calling a custom BTP CF agent from Joule.

 

Any guidance, documentation links, or practical experiences would be very helpful.

entry.title: Question on GTI License Requirements for China Golden Tax Integration Scenarios
entry.content:
Hi SAP colleagues,
I would like to get your advice on the license requirements related to SAP Golden Tax Interface (GTI) in China localization.
We are currently evaluating different integration approaches between SAP and the Chinese Golden Tax System, and I would like to confirm whether my understanding of the licensing implications is correct.
■ Background
We assume that SAP SD billing data is transferred to the Golden Tax Interface (GTI), where data validation, splitting, and consolidation are performed before invoice issuance.
■ Integration Scenarios
1. Manual Integration
SAP outputs billing data in the required format of the Golden Tax System.
The data is then manually processed and uploaded into the Golden Tax System to issue invoices.
2. Integration via Third-Party System
SAP sends data in the format required by a third-party vendor, and the third-party system handles the integration with the Golden Tax System.
3. Direct Integration
SAP directly sends data to the Golden Tax System via APIs/interfaces for invoice issuance.
■ Questions
1. For Scenario 1 (manual integration): If we use standard GTI functions in SAP (such as data extraction, validation, splitting, or consolidation), would a GTI-related license still be required?
2. For Scenarios 2 and 3: Is it correct to understand that a GTI (or related) license is required whenever SAP is involved in the integration process?
3. In an S/4HANA environment:
When using solutions such as
- SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC)
- China Tax e-Invoice Management how do these relate to GTI licensing?
Are additional licenses required?
Any insights or experience sharing would be greatly appreciated.
Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Running KILL
entry.content: I don't know much about the gdb/programming, but if syslogshold doesn't return any process and sp_who doesn't return other spids likely to be the 'gdb' process then the process should just not exist in ASE. From ASE perspective you can try restarting the server if possible.

entry.title: Re: CLSD SDI OSS note clarification needed
entry.content:
Good day!

You can read KBA 1991171 for more information.

It mentioned that Orders with status CLSD will be selected and processed, but processing will stop early, like in the single transaction due to the fact the status forbids further processing.

Solution: Please set status DLFL (Deletion flag) to the orders instead. (Report PPARCHP1 can be used for production orders, report PRARCHP1 can be used for process orders.)

Hope this help!

entry.title: How to add Material Group Description to Pick List Form (FDP_PM_MAINTENANCEORDER_SRV) in S/4HANA Pub
entry.content:
Hi Community,

I have a requirement from a business user to add the Material Group Description alongside the Material Group Code on the printed Pick List form template in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Currently, only the Material Group Code is available in the standard data stream, and the description is missing. I am trying to find a cloud-compliant workaround to achieve this, but I have run into several dead ends with the standard extensibility framework:

  1. Custom Fields / BAdI: I tried creating a Custom Field, but under the Form Templates tab, there are no maintenance-related form templates available to extend. Additionally, I couldn't find a suitable BAdI for Maintenance Orders or Operations to update a custom field value at runtime.

  2. Data Source Extensibility: I attempted to extend the pre-delivered data source via the Custom Fields and Logic app, but FDP_PM_MAINTENANCEORDER_SRV is not available in the extensible data sources list.

  3. Custom CDS Binding: Since standard Form Data Providers (FDPs) cannot have their underlying gateway services swapped out at the configuration level, I cannot bind a new custom CDS view directly to this standard output configuration.

Given that this is S/4HANA Public Cloud, we cannot modify standard CDS views or write custom ABAP on the core backend.

Has anyone successfully brought the Material Group Description (or any other missing text description) into a PM pick list under these constraints? Are there any specific configuration workarounds, alternative extensible standard data providers for PM components, or standard APIs we can leverage within the standard output management framework?

Any insights or alternative approaches would be highly appreciated!

Regards,

Tri

entry.title: FB03 authorization based on Cost Center when called from S_ALR_87013611
entry.content:
環境：

  • SAP S/4HANA

  • 用戶沒有 FB03 交易授權。

  • 但是，使用者可以從報表 S_ALR_87013611 向下鑽取並顯示會計憑證。

目前授權設計：

  • 成本中心授權由 K_CCA 和 K_REPO_CCA 等授權物件控制。

  • 使用者只能在類似以下的報告中看到已授權的成本中心：

    • S_ALR_87013611

    • KSB1

要求：
當使用者深入查看會計憑證時，系統應檢查憑證中的所有行項目。

如果文件中包含使用者無權查看的任何成本中心，則不應顯示該文件。

問題：

  1. 是否存在可以根據成本中心授權來控制 FB03 文件顯示的標準 SAP 授權對象？

  2. 有沒有人使用 BTE、BAdI 或用戶出口實現了這個需求？

  3. 當透過報表鑽取而不是直接執行 FB03 來存取文件時，建議在哪個增強點進行文件顯示授權檢查？

  4. 如何處理同時包含已授權和未授權成本中心的文件？

如有任何不清楚的地方，請提出建議。謝謝！
